structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health record (EHR) system spanning 36 months. Patient cohort: all patients discharged from inpatient care during the study period. Raw data includes unstructured clinician notes (admission notes, progress notes, discharge summaries) and structured EHR fields (demographics, comorbidities, lab values, medications, vital signs). Readmission outcome defined as any inpatient readmission within 30 days of discharge, obtained from institutional admission logs.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: readmitted within 30 days of discharge (1) or not readmitted within 30 days (0). Readmission is operationally defined as any inpatient hospital admission occurring ≥24 hours and ≤30 days after discharge from the index hospitalization. Same institutional system required for label accuracy; transfers to other health systems are not captured and represent a known limitation.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by discharge cohort. Months 1–24 (first two years) used for training; months 25–30 (months 6 months) used for validation; months 31–36 (final 6 months) used as held-out test. Temporal splitting is essential because: (1) it prevents information leakage—the model cannot access future patient outcomes or clinical notes from patients not yet seen; (2) it reflects deployment reality, where the model must generalize to newly discharged patients; (3) it avoids artificial performance inflation from temporal patterns. Within each temporal block, records are stratified by readmission outcome (binary stratification) to preserve label distribution across splits. No patient appears in multiple splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (text tokenizers, vocabulary, scaling parameters, imputation statistics, normalization coefficients) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). Fitted transformers are then applied without refitting to validation (months 25–30) and test sets (months 31–36). Structured EHR features (demographics, comorbidities, etc.) undergo standard scaling fit on training data only. Clinician notes undergo tokenization and encoding using a fixed vocabulary derived from the training corpus. This protocol prevents leakage of validation and test information into the feature space.
structured_choices.features: Transformer baseline (hyp_040 variant): raw or minimally preprocessed clinician notes (discharge summary, admission note, and final progress note concatenated); structured EHR features (age, sex, Charlson comorbidity index, primary ICD-10 diagnosis, admission type, length of stay, number of prior admissions in past year). Logistic regression baseline: identical structured EHR feature set (no notes). For the transformer model, notes are tokenized using a pre-trained BERT tokenizer, truncated to 512 tokens, and passed through a fine-tuned transformer encoder. Structured features are concatenated with the transformer's [CLS] token representation. No feature engineering is applied to notes; they are used as-is to isolate the contribution of the pre-trained model's learned representations. Structured features are standard-scaled (fit on training data).
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model: BERT (or similar pre-trained transformer) fine-tuned on the EHR notes+structured data for 30-day readmission prediction. Architecture: BERT encoder → [CLS] token pooling → concatenate with structured feature embeddings → 2-layer MLP (hidden dim 128, dropout 0.2) → sigmoid output. Pre-trained weights initialized from a general domain transformer (e.g., BERT-base); fine-tuning occurs only on the training set. Hyperparameters: learning rate 2e-5, batch size 32, epochs 3–5 (early stopping on validation loss). Training uses binary cross-entropy loss.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on structured EHR features only (no clinician notes). Same structured features as transformer model (age, sex, Charlson index, diagnosis, admission type, length of stay, prior admission count), standard-scaled fit on training data. Logistic regression hyperparameter tuning: L2 regularization strength (C parameter) tuned via grid search on validation data (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100}). This baseline is fair because: (1) it uses the same data modality (structured EHR fields) across both models, isolating the contribution of notes; (2) it receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search); (3) it is evaluated under identical conditions (same train/val/test split, same preprocessing pipeline); (4) logistic regression is a standard, well-understood clinical ML baseline. The comparison directly tests whether transformer-based notes improve over structured data alone.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: In the operational context, clinicians need to rank patients by readmission risk to allocate preventive resources (e.g., post-discharge calls, care coordination). AUROC measures the model's ability to discriminate between readmitted and non-readmitted patients across all possible decision thresholds, making it appropriate for risk stratification. Unlike accuracy or F1-score, AUROC is insensitive to class imbalance (readmission is typically 10–20% of discharges). Secondary stakeholder concern is recall at a fixed threshold (e.g., recall ≥90%) to ensure at-risk patients are not missed; this is measured separately but does not serve as the primary metric to avoid threshold selection bias during model selection.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC (particularly important if readmission is rare, <10%)
Sensitivity (true positive rate) at 90% threshold — ensures high-risk patients are captured
Specificity — measures false alarm rate among non-readmitted patients
Calibration error (expected calibration error, ECE) — models used for risk estimation must be well-calibrated; miscalibrated models mislead clinicians about absolute risk
Balanced accuracy — provides secondary confirmation of performance across classes
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. For transformer: early stopping on validation AUROC (patience=2 epochs). For logistic regression: C parameter selected via grid search maximizing validation AUROC. The hyperparameter configuration achieving highest validation AUROC is used for final test evaluation. Validation data is never used for training; it is held out exclusively for model selection.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36) is touched exactly once, at the very end of the experiment, after all hyperparameter tuning and model selection are complete. Final model checkpoints (best transformer weights, best logistic regression weights) are applied to the test set without any refitting or adjustment. Metrics (AUROC, secondary metrics) are computed on test predictions and reported as the final performance estimate. No iteration or tuning occurs based on test results.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Patient demographics and socioeconomic status confound readmission risk. Controls: (1) Both models receive identical demographic features (age, sex); these are visible to both, so neither model has advantage. (2) Stratified analysis: readmission rates are reported separately by age group, sex, and Charlson comorbidity category to expose any subgroup disparities. (3) Confounding-adjusted comparison: logistic regression model is also trained with demographic interactions (age × sex, comorbidity × admission type) to check whether simple baseline can capture confounding; if transformer model improvement persists after this adjustment, the improvement is less likely due to unmeasured confounding. (4) Limitation: unmeasured confounds (e.g., social support, insurance status) cannot be fully controlled; this is noted in scope. The experiment establishes that the transformer model predicts readmission better using available data; it does not establish causation or that readmission risk itself is caused by any feature.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Clinical Notes for Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Labeling

Data is sourced from a large EHR system covering 36 months of inpatient discharges. The cohort includes all patients discharged during this period. Raw data comprises: (a) unstructured clinician notes (discharge summaries, admission notes, final progress notes), and (b) structured EHR fields (age, sex, Charlson comorbidity index, primary diagnosis, admission type, length of stay, count of prior admissions in the past 12 months). The binary outcome is defined as readmission within 30 days of discharge (1 = readmitted within ≥24 hours and ≤30 days; 0 = not readmitted within 30 days). Readmission status is obtained from institutional admission logs; transfers to external facilities are not captured and represent a known data limitation.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

A temporal split with stratified sampling is used:
- **Training set (Months 1–24):** Used to fit all preprocessing transformers and to train both models.
- **Validation set (Months 25–30):** Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection; never used for training.
- **Test set (Months 31–36):** Held out completely; used once at the end for final evaluation.

Temporal splitting is essential because it prevents information leakage—the model cannot use future patient outcomes or clinical context from patients not yet discharged. This mirrors the deployment scenario where the model must generalize to newly discharged patients. Within each temporal block, records are stratified by readmission outcome (binary stratification) to preserve label class distribution. No patient appears in multiple splits.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only and applied (without refitting) to validation and test sets:

- **Clinician notes:** Notes are tokenized using a pre-trained BERT tokenizer, truncated to 512 tokens, and encoded as token IDs. A fixed vocabulary is derived from the training corpus. Notes are not manually cleaned or annotated; minimal preprocessing isolates the contribution of the transformer's learned representations.
- **Structured EHR features:** Age, sex, Charlson comorbidity index, primary diagnosis, admission type, length of stay, and prior admission count are standard-scaled (mean centering and unit variance) using statistics fit on training data.
- **Feature set for transformer model:** Concatenated clinician notes + structured EHR features.
- **Feature set for baseline model:** Structured EHR features only (no notes).

### 4. Model Specifications

**Transformer Model:**
BERT (pre-trained, general domain) fine-tuned on the training data. Architecture: BERT encoder → [CLS] token pooling → concatenation with structured feature embeddings → 2-layer MLP (128 hidden units, dropout 0.2) → sigmoid output for binary prediction. Hyperparameters: learning rate 2×10⁻⁵, batch size 32, epochs 3–5 with early stopping on validation loss (patience 2 epochs). Loss function: binary cross-entropy.

**Baseline Model:**
Logistic regression trained on structured EHR features alone (no notes). Hyperparameter C (L2 regularization strength) tuned via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100}, selecting C that maximizes validation AUROC. The baseline is fair because both models receive the same structured features, identical preprocessing, equivalent tuning effort, and identical evaluation conditions. This isolates the contribution of fine-tuned transformer notes.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set (months 25–30) is used exclusively for model selection. For the transformer: early stopping monitors validation AUROC. For logistic regression: grid search selects C based on validation AUROC. The hyperparameter configuration achieving the highest validation AUROC is locked in and applied to the test set. No further tuning occurs after validation-set evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: Clinicians use readmission risk scores to allocate preventive resources; AUROC measures discrimination ability across all thresholds, is insensitive to class imbalance, and aligns with the operational goal of stratifying patients by risk.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision-Recall AUC (important if readmission is rare).
- Sensitivity at 90% threshold (ensures high-risk patients are identified).
- Specificity (false alarm rate).
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE) — clinicians must trust absolute risk estimates.
- Balanced accuracy — secondary confirmation across classes.

### 7. Confounding and Stratified Analysis

Patient demographics and socioeconomic status confound readmission. Controls: (1) Both models receive identical demographic features (age, sex), eliminating model-specific advantages. (2) Stratified evaluation: readmission rates and model performance are reported separately by age group, sex, and comorbidity category to expose disparities. (3) Confounding-adjusted baseline: logistic regression is also trained with demographic interactions (age × sex, comorbidity × admission type) to test whether a richer baseline closes the performance gap; if the transformer model outperforms even this, the improvement is less attributable to unmeasured confounding. (4) Scope: unmeasured confounds (social support, insurance) cannot be controlled; the experiment establishes that the transformer predicts readmission better using available data but does not establish causation.

### 8. Summary of Claims

This design tests whether a transformer model fine-tuned on clinician notes + structured data outperforms logistic regression on structured data alone for 30-day readmission prediction. The temporal split prevents leakage. Fair baseline tuning, identical evaluation, and stratified analysis account for confounds and ensure a rigorous comparison.
